learn.title: Harvesting the Power of Ocean Waves
learn.intro: Wave energy is a massive, untapped resource that offers a more predictable power source than wind or solar. By capturing the kinetic energy of the ocean, we can diversify the global energy mix and provide reliable power to coastal communities, reducing reliance on fossil fuels and lowering carbon emissions from the maritime sector.
learn.c1_text: These devices act like giant floating buoys. As waves move them up and down, they drive a hydraulic piston to generate power. Think of it like a bicycle pump: the movement pushes fluid to turn a turbine, converting mechanical motion into electricity.
learn.c2_text: These long, segmented structures float parallel to the wave direction. As the wave bends the joints, the relative motion generates energy. It is similar to a folding accordion that generates a spark every time it bends, capturing energy across a wider surface area.
learn.c3_text: India's 7,500km coastline offers immense potential for marine energy. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy is exploring pilot projects to integrate wave energy into the national grid, complementing the country's massive solar and wind targets to ensure 24/7 green power.
learn.stat1: Ocean energy potential: Terawatts of global capacity
learn.stat2: Wave energy density: Higher than wind or solar
learn.stat4: Global marine energy growth: 15% CAGR projected

learn.title: India's EV Policy and PM e-DRIVE Scheme
learn.intro: India is rapidly transitioning to electric mobility to combat urban air pollution and reduce oil imports. The PM e-DRIVE scheme is a cornerstone of this shift, providing the financial incentives and infrastructure needed to make EVs affordable for the masses and accelerate the adoption of electric two-wheelers and buses.
learn.c1_text: This scheme provides direct subsidies to buyers to lower the upfront cost of EVs. It acts like a discount coupon from the government, making an electric scooter nearly the same price as a petrol one, encouraging millions to switch to clean transport.
learn.c2_text: Policy focuses on deploying thousands of public charging stations. By building a 'fuel station' network for electricity, the government removes 'range anxiety,' the fear that a vehicle will run out of power before reaching a charger during long trips.
learn.c3_text: States like Maharashtra and Delhi offer additional road tax waivers and registration fee exemptions. This layered approach means a buyer in Delhi gets both central and state benefits, drastically reducing the Total Cost of Ownership compared to ICE vehicles.
learn.stat1: PM e-DRIVE budget: Thousands of crores allocated
learn.stat3: India EV sales: Millions of units annually
learn.stat4: FAME-II impact: Lakhs of EVs subsidized

learn.title: The Future of Solid-State Batteries
learn.intro: Current lithium-ion batteries use liquid electrolytes, which can be flammable and limit energy capacity. Solid-state batteries replace this liquid with a solid material, promising a revolution in safety, charging speed, and range, effectively solving the biggest hurdles to the mass adoption of electric vehicles.
learn.c1_text: Instead of a liquid 'soup' that carries ions, these use a solid ceramic or polymer layer. It is like replacing a leaky water pipe with a solid copper wire; it is more stable and prevents the internal short-circuits that cause fires.
learn.c2_text: Solid-state batteries can pack more energy into the same space. This means a battery of the same size could potentially double the range of a car, allowing a vehicle to travel 800km on a single charge instead of 400km.
learn.c3_text: The National Programme on Advanced Chemistry Cell (ACC) Battery Storage aims to localize battery production. By developing solid-state tech locally, India seeks to reduce dependence on imports and lower the cost of EVs for the domestic market.
learn.stat1: Energy density increase: Up to 2x vs Li-ion
learn.stat2: Charging time: Potential reduction to <15 mins

learn.title: How Electric Vehicles Work
learn.intro: Electric vehicles (EVs) replace the internal combustion engine with an electric motor and a battery pack. This shift simplifies the drivetrain, removes tailpipe emissions, and introduces new ways to recover energy, making transport significantly more efficient and sustainable for our cities.
learn.c1_text: The motor uses magnetic fields to create rotation. It is like a high-tech spinning top; when electricity flows, the magnets push against each other to turn the wheels instantly, providing 100% of the torque from a standstill.
learn.c2_text: When you brake, the motor runs in reverse, acting as a generator that sends energy back into the battery. It is like a 'energy recycler' that captures the heat usually wasted during braking and turns it back into miles.
learn.c3_text: India is adopting multiple standards like CCS and GB/T. With the rise of Tata Power and Jio-bp, charging hubs are appearing in malls and highways, mirroring the convenience of traditional petrol pumps across Indian cities.
learn.stat1: Efficiency: EVs convert >85% of energy to motion
learn.stat2: ICE efficiency: Only ~20% of fuel energy used
learn.stat3: India EV growth: 150% YoY growth in 2W/3W

learn.title: Offshore Wind Farms and Grid Integration
learn.intro: Offshore wind harnesses stronger, more consistent winds found at sea compared to land. By placing massive turbines in the ocean, we can generate gigawatts of clean power near coastal cities, reducing the need for long-distance transmission lines and utilizing vast maritime spaces.
learn.c1_text: Fixed turbines are bolted to the seabed in shallow water. Floating turbines use moored platforms for deep water. It is like the difference between a house on a foundation and a boat anchored in a bay; both stay put but use different supports.
learn.c2_text: Power is collected by 'array cables' and sent to an offshore substation, which steps up the voltage for long-distance travel to the shore. This ensures the electricity doesn't 'leak' away as heat during its journey to the city.
learn.c3_text: India has identified high-potential zones off the coasts of Gujarat and Tamil Nadu. The government is developing a bidding framework to attract global developers to build India's first large-scale offshore wind farms to meet its 500 GW RE goal.
learn.stat1: Turbine size: Some reach 260m in height
learn.stat2: Capacity factor: Offshore wind is 40-50% efficient
learn.stat4: Global capacity: Over 60 GW installed globally

learn.title: Green Steel and Hydrogen Production
learn.intro: Steel production is one of the hardest sectors to decarbonize because it relies on coal to remove oxygen from iron ore. Green steel replaces coal with green hydrogen, eliminating CO2 emissions and transforming one of the world's most polluting industries into a sustainable one.
learn.c1_text: In a traditional blast furnace, carbon (coal) removes oxygen. In green steel, hydrogen does the job. Instead of releasing CO2, the only byproduct is water vapor. It is like replacing a smoky fire with a clean steam cleaner.
learn.c2_text: To make 'green' hydrogen, electricity from wind or solar splits water (H2O) into hydrogen and oxygen. This process requires massive amounts of renewable energy to ensure the hydrogen is truly carbon-free from start to finish.
learn.c3_text: The National Green Hydrogen Mission aims for 5 MMT of production by 2030. Indian steel giants like TATA Steel are exploring hydrogen-ready furnaces to ensure India's infrastructure remains competitive in a low-carbon global market.
learn.stat1: Steel sector: ~7-9% of global CO2 emissions
learn.stat2: Green H2 goal: 5 MMT per year by 2030
learn.stat3: Energy use: Steel uses ~6% of global energy
